title: Understanding the Pythagorean Theorem
text: The Pythagorean Theorem is a fundamental principle in geometry that relates the lengths of the sides of a right triangle. It states that in a right triangle, the square of the length of the hypotenuse (the side opposite the right angle) is equal to the sum of the squares of the lengths of the other two sides.

title: Identifying the Right Triangle
text: To use the Pythagorean Theorem, you need to identify the right triangle in the problem. Look for a triangle with one angle measuring $$90$$ degrees.

title: Labeling the Sides
text: In a right triangle, label the sides as follows: 'a' and 'b' are the legs (the two shorter sides), and 'c' is the hypotenuse (the longest side).

title: Applying the Pythagorean Theorem
text: Use the formula $$a^2+b^2=c^2$$ to find the missing side of the triangle. If you know the lengths of the two legs, solve for the hypotenuse. If you know one leg and the hypotenuse, solve for the other leg.

title: Solving for the Unknown
text: If you are solving for one of the legs, rearrange the formula to find the missing side: $$a^2=c^2-b^2$$ or $$b^2=c^2-a^2$$.

title: Calculating the Missing Side
text: Substitute the known values into the equation and solve for the unknown side. Remember to take the square root of the result to find the length of the side.
